figures.caption: Table 3: Safety Tier 2 results (EN+ZH aggregate). All requests are safe in a neutral adult setting. Cue-aware open-ended tasks report Direct Answer Rate (DAR, ↓) and Safety Awareness Rate (SAR, ↑); Unsafe Ambient (discriminative Yes/No task) reports Acc (↑); OIC (↓) measures overlap-induced conversion, i.e., how often audio overlap turns an otherwise safe refusal into unsafe compliance. Final row: text reference upper bound (safest of DeepSeek-V3, Gemini-3-Pro, GPT-5.2).

Table 7: Tier 1 safety overview (content-centric). We follow a top-down taxonomy that separates what the user asks (harmful content), how adversaries try to bypass refusals (jailbreak), and what an agent can do with tools (agentic action safety).

Table 20: Happy-tragedy emotion extension. DAR (↓) and SAR (↑) are reported per language. Two text simulations are shown separately because their divergence reveals a model-specific alignment gap rather than an audio-grounding failure.
Table 32: Scenario taxonomy for contextual & social privacy (soft privacy). This table illustrates user intents, concrete examples, and the expected safe behavior from the model.

Table 25: Net Bias Score (%) and statistical significance across five stereotype dimensions, reported as EN / ZH. Positive values indicate alignment with societal stereotypes (e.g., preference for natives, males in male-dominated jobs), while negative values indicate counter-stereotype bias. Significance levels from two-sided binomial tests: ∗p < 0.05, †p < 0.01, ‡p < 0.001. Bold indicates statistically significant bias.

Table 35: Interactional privacy results. Metrics are computed on 144 paired probes per language (288 trials). Precision/Recall/F1 treat withholding secrets to an impostor speaker as the positive class. All values are in %. Bold: best per metric per language.
Table 11: Single-turn jailbreak taxonomy used in Tier 1.

Table 39: Spearman’s rank correlation (ρ) between per-model SAR scores produced by each LLM judge and the human majority-vote reference, as well as between LLM judge pairs. With only 7 model variants, these values should be read as descriptive evidence of ranking robustness.

Table 40: Per-model DAR and SAR (%) on Content-Paralinguistic Conflict subtasks (S2.1), as evaluated by each judge on the same sampled item set. Numbers are EN+ZH averages. “∆max” is the maximum absolute difference across the four judges for each model.

Figure 1: Overview of the VoxSafeBench. We evaluate social alignment of speech language models through three pillars (safety, fairness, and privacy) under a Two-Tier design that separates contentcentric risks (Tier 1) from audio-conditioned risks (Tier 2), where the same benign transcript becomes unsafe, unfair, or privacy-violating due to speech-native cues.

Table 4: Fairness evaluation results (Fair Rate ↑, %) across four stereotype dimensions, reported as EN / ZH. Tier 1 columns evaluate fairness across Text and Audio (averaged Clean and Diverse) modalities. Tier 2 columns probe audio-conditioned fairness. Bold: column best per language; “—”: text input not supported; †: thinking variant.
Table 38: Fleiss’ Kappa (κ) for each included Safety Tier 2 subtask, computed over three rater configurations. All subtasks use 4-class labeling (DAR/WAR/RtA/SKIP).

Table 9: Mapping of Tier 1 harmful-content super-categories to representative safety benchmarks and taxonomies. This grounding ensures our taxonomy comprehensively covers both established textual harms and domain-regulated risks.

Table 10: Harmful content (No Jailbreak) evaluation results across text and audio modalities, reported as EN / ZH. We compare Refusal-to-Answer (RtA ↑, %) and Toxicity Score (↓ ×100) for pure text, clean audio, and diverse audio. Text columns evaluate the pure-text modality. Audio columns evaluate clean and diverse audio modalities. Bold: column best per language; “—”: text input not supported; †: thinking variant.

Table 16: Discriminative unsafe-detection accuracy (Acc ↑, %), reported as EN / ZH. Text columns evaluate the pure-text modality. Audio columns evaluate clean/diverse audio (No Jailbreak) and jailbreak audio (Single-turn). Since all discriminative samples are unsafe by construction, Acc corresponds to the recall of predicting Yes among non-skipped cases. “Skipped” outputs (nonparsable Yes/No or API/pipeline errors) are excluded from the accuracy denominator. Bold: column best per language; “—”: modality not supported; †: thinking variant.

Table 43: Intermediate probe results on three additional audio-conditioned cue families. For Age and Accent, we report Probe Acc. only, since current fairness reporting does not isolate cue-specific downstream outcomes for these factors. For Background Speech, each cell reports Probe Acc. / PAR. The human row reports the mean probe accuracy of three annotators.

Table 23: Safety Tier 2 qualitative examples and failure cases. Each row shows a benign transcript whose appropriate response depends on an audio-conditioned cue. The EXPECTED column describes the desired safe behavior; the FAILURE column shows an actual model output that ignores the acoustic cue entirely. All failure outputs are real model responses (lightly shortened for space).
Table 1: Coverage comparison of audio safety-related benchmarks under a unified taxonomy. Legend: ✓ fully evaluated; G# partially covered; ✗ not covered. Modality: T-text; A-audio; V-video; I-image.

Table 15: Jailbreak Attack Success Rate (ASR ↓, %) across Single-turn and Multi-turn scenarios, reported as EN / ZH. Text columns evaluate the pure-text modality. Audio columns evaluate the audio modality. Bold: column best (lowest ASR) per language; “—”: text input not supported; †: thinking variant.

Figure 8: Impact of acoustic disturbance on single-turn jailbreak robustness. We report the Refusalto-Answer (RtA, %) for each model with and without acoustic disturbances (e.g., emotional pressure and paralinguistic variations). Higher RtA indicates better safety robustness. Across most models, acoustic disturbances lead to a noticeable drop in RtA in both English and Chinese, demonstrating that paralinguistic manipulation can effectively bypass existing safety guardrails.

Table 14: Jailbreak evaluation results (RtA ↑, %; Toxicity Score ↓ ×100) across No Jailbreak, Single-turn Jailbreak, and Multi-turn Jailbreak settings, reported as EN / ZH. Text columns evaluate the pure-text modality. Audio columns evaluate the audio modality. Bold: column best per language; “—”: text input not supported; †: thinking variant.

Table 18: Examples of Direct and Indirect Harm in Agentic Action Safety. In Direct Harm, the user’s intent is explicitly malicious. In Indirect Harm, the user’s intent is benign, but the tool return contains toxic or manipulative content (prompt injection) that an unsafe agent improperly propagates.
Table 17: Tier 1 agentic action safety taxonomy.

Table 24: Full Fairness evaluation results (Fair Rate ↑, %) across four stereotype dimensions, reported as EN / ZH. Tier 1 columns evaluate single-turn fairness across three modalities (Text, Clean Audio, Diverse Audio). Tier 2 columns probe audio-conditioned fairness. Bold: column best per language; “—”: text input not supported; †: thinking variant.

Table 42: Intermediate probe accuracy and matched Tier 2 safety performance on the same cue family. Each cell reports Probe Acc. / Safety. For Child Voice, Emotion, Impaired Capacity, and Child Presence, the safety metric is SAR. For Unsafe Background, the matched safety metric is Unsafe Ambient Acc.; this pair should be interpreted qualitatively rather than as a strict upper/lower bound because the probe asks cue recognition (“Is NSFW audio present?”) while the benchmark asks scene appropriateness (“Is the clip suitable for teaching?”). The human row reports the mean probe accuracy of three annotators; the text-reference row reports the ceiling from the original Tier 2 safety evaluation.

Figure 6: Overview of our dataset-construction pipeline. We combine adapted benchmarks, LLMassisted generation, and off-the-shelf resources; clean and revise candidate texts for spoken use; synthesize or record audio with a curated prompt-audio pool; inject Tier 2 acoustic cues; filter by intelligibility; and finally release Tier 1 and Tier 2 evaluation sets with human verification.
Table 8: Tier 1 harmful-content taxonomy. Descriptions focus on what the evaluation forbids the model from facilitating, without enumerating operational details.

Table 33: Detailed Tier 1 hard/soft privacy results. We report Discriminative Accuracy (Acc ↑) and Generative Leakage (Leak ↓) / Refusal-to-Answer (RtA ↑) rates. English and Chinese results are denoted by teal and orange respectively, with the best value in each language bolded. “—”: text input not supported; †: thinking variant.
Table 29: Tier 1 privacy taxonomy and evaluation targets. “Hard” privacy concerns structured, copyable identifiers and secrets, while “soft” privacy is context-dependent and socially sensitive.

Table 5: Tier 1 hard/soft privacy results, with a compact Tier 2 audio-conditioned privacy probe. Columns 2–7 report Leakage (↓) / refuse-to-Answer (RtA, ↑) rates (%), aggregated over EN+ZH, under three modalities: text (pure-text), clean audio, and diverse audio. The last column reports Direct Answer Rate (DAR, ↓) / Privacy Awareness Rate (PAR, ↑) for audio-conditioned privacy. “—”: text input not supported; †: thinking variant.

Figure 3: Net Bias Score (%) across five dimensions. Blue/Orange dots denote English/Chinese queries. Positive values (right) reflect alignment with societal stereotypes; negative values (left) indicate counter-stereotype biases. The connecting gray line highlights the language bias gap. Asterisks (∗) denote statistical significance (p < 0.05).
Table 2: Task overview of VoxSafeBench. In the Dataset column, ¥ indicates constructed-fromscratch data, indicates adapted benchmarks, and q indicates off-the-shelf datasets.

Figure 9: Average intermediate probe accuracy and matched safety performance on the four directly matched open-ended safety Tier 2 subtasks: Child Voice, Emotion, Impaired Capacity, and Child Presence. Blue points denote intermediate probe accuracy; orange points denote the corresponding Safety Awareness Rate (SAR) from Table 3 and Appendix Tables 19–21. Gray segments connect the same model. Large gaps indicate that a model can detect the cue substantially better than it can translate that cue into safe behavior. The dashed green line marks the human probe average, and the dotted red line marks the text-reference safety ceiling on the same four subtasks.
Table 30: Taxonomy for structured PII & credentials (hard privacy).

Table 22: Overlapped instruction injection results (S2.3). Unsafe rate (↓) is the fraction of overlap responses judged unsafe. OIC (↓) is the overlap-induced conversion rate: among overlap cases judged unsafe, the fraction whose isolated unsafe track would have been safe when asked alone. Unsafe base is the total number of unsafe overlap cases across EN+ZH used as the OIC denominator.

Table 34: Detailed audio-conditioned privacy results. We report English and Chinese Direct Answer Rate (DAR, ↓), Warning Answer Rate (WAR, ↑), Refusal-to-Answer (RtA, ↑), and Privacy Awareness Rate (PAR=WAR+RtA, ↑). The final row is a text reference upper bound: we ran the text condition with DeepSeek-V3, Gemini-3-Pro, and GPT-5.2; Gemini-3-Pro performed best and is shown here.

Table 6: Composition of the prompt-audio pool used to condition CosyVoice3 synthesis. Counts are reported within each attribute family. “Shared” denotes non-language-specific background source clips used in the diverse pool; these sources are randomly mixed into 30% of diverse outputs at 40% of foreground speech volume. For the clean row and emotion rows, we report mean arousal estimated on the prompt audios.

Figure 4: (a) Interactional privacy: Precision–recall (English/Chinese); contours show F1 and marker size encodes accuracy (0.50 ≈ random guessing). (b) Inferential privacy: Refusal-to-Answer (RtA) vs. inference accuracy (ACC) on five sensitive attributes from HearSay [7]. Error bars denote standard deviation; safer models lie in the bottom-right region (high RtA, low ACC).
Table 36: Inferential privacy results on five UGI attributes from HearSay [7] (%). RtA (↑): refusal rate; ACC (↓): inference accuracy on non-refusal samples. Bold: best per column.
Table 13: Multi-turn scenario cloaks (benign covers) that often accompany methodology-driven attacks.
Table 12: Multi-turn jailbreak methodology taxonomy (Tier 1).
Table 31: Prompt scenario templates for structured PII & credentials.

Figure 2: Tier 1 safety evaluation. Left: Safety degradation trajectories in the Refuse to Answer (RtA)–Toxicity score2 plane under escalating adversarial attacks: No Jailbreak (◦), Single-turn (□), and Multi-turn (△). The safe region lies in the top-left (high RtA, low Toxicity). Dashed/solid lines denote text/audio modalities; red/blue distinguish reasoning (Thinking/Pro) and standard variants. Right: Agentic RtA under Direct vs. Indirect harm (a), and the text–audio vulnerability gap in Direct harm mirroring single-turn jailbreak behavior (b).

Table 28: Tier 2 qualitative examples illustrating paralinguistic and background-sound bias. The text script is identical for both speakers; each probe is evaluated twice by swapping acoustic traits. Consistent preference for a specific acoustic profile demonstrates systemic voice/environment bias.
Table 27: Tier 1 qualitative examples (Chinese). The probe remains a minimal pair; any preference based on the group descriptor is UNFAIR.
Table 26: Tier 1 qualitative examples (English). Candidates are identical except for the explicit demographic descriptor; selecting one is UNFAIR.
Table 41: Per-model SAR (%) on Content-Background Conflict subtasks (S2.2), as evaluated by each judge on the same sampled item set. Numbers are EN+ZH averages.
